name: Ohio
subtitle: 269 species — built from 12 years of eBird checklists
source: Species list and frequency from eBird bar chart data.

species.name: Canada Goose
species.region: Reported on 54.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Once purely migratory, urban populations are now year-round residents across most of the continent. Highly social, fiercely territorial when nesting, and famous for the V-formation flight.
species.male: Black head and neck with a bold white chinstrap. Brown body, pale breast. Ganders are larger but plumage is identical.
species.female: Identical to the male but noticeably smaller. She does the incubating while he stands guard nearby.
species.habitat: Lakes, ponds, rivers, golf courses, park lawns, and farm fields. Needs open water beside short grass for grazing.

species.name: Mallard
species.region: Reported on 39.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The world's most abundant dabbling duck and the ancestor of nearly every domestic duck breed. Thrives in almost any watery habitat, from wilderness marsh to city retention pond.
species.male: Iridescent green head, yellow bill, chestnut breast set off by a white neck ring, grey body, curled black tail feathers.
species.female: Mottled brown with an orange bill blotched in black. Both sexes show a blue speculum bordered in white.
species.habitat: Extraordinarily adaptable — ponds, lakes, rivers, marshes, flooded fields, and urban parks.

species.name: Great Blue Heron
species.region: Reported on 34.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The largest heron in North America, standing over four feet tall, yet weighing only five or six pounds thanks to hollow bones. Hunts by standing motionless, then striking with a spear-like bill.
species.male: Slate-grey body, white face with a black plume trailing from the eye, a dagger-like yellow bill, and rusty thighs.
species.female: Identical to the male in plumage, slightly smaller on average. Both sexes grow ornate chest plumes when breeding.
species.habitat: Any water at all — lakes, rivers, marshes, estuaries, even wet fields. Nests colonially in tall trees.

species.name: Ring-billed Gull
species.region: Reported on 30.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The quintessential parking-lot gull — abundant, bold, and thoroughly at home around people. Often the gull begging for fries a hundred miles from any coast.
species.male: White head and body, pale grey back, and a yellow bill crossed by a neat black ring. Yellow legs.
species.female: Identical to the male. Takes three years to reach full adult plumage, so mottled brown youngsters are common.
species.habitat: Lakes, rivers, coasts, landfills, parking lots, and farm fields. Extremely adaptable.

species.name: Killdeer
species.region: Reported on 28.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Famous for the broken-wing act — it drags a 'crippled' wing away from the nest until the predator follows, then flies off perfectly fine. Nests on bare gravel, including parking lots and flat roofs.
species.male: Brown above, white below, with two crisp black breast bands and a rusty rump that flashes in flight.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both parents perform the distraction display, though only the female incubates.
species.habitat: Open bare ground of any kind — gravel lots, airports, farm fields, shorelines, and playing fields.

species.name: Great Egret
species.region: Reported on 26.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Nearly wiped out in the 1800s when its breeding plumes were worth more than gold to the hat trade. The outrage helped launch the Audubon Society, and the bird became its symbol.
species.male: All white with a heavy yellow bill and black legs and feet. Grows long, gauzy back plumes when breeding.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both sexes display the trailing plumes during courtship.
species.habitat: Marshes, ponds, tidal flats, and flooded fields. Nests in colonies, often with other herons.

species.name: Double-crested Cormorant
species.region: Reported on 24.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Its feathers are deliberately wettable, which cuts buoyancy and makes it a superb underwater pursuit diver — but it must then stand with wings spread to dry, the classic cormorant pose.
species.male: All black with a hooked bill and a patch of bare orange skin at the throat. Iridescent green sheen in good light.
species.female: Identical to the male. The paired head tufts that give the bird its name are only briefly present in breeding season.
species.habitat: Lakes, rivers, reservoirs, and coasts. Nests colonially in trees or on rocky islands.

species.name: Wood Duck
species.region: Reported on 17.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Often called the most beautiful duck in North America. Unique among waterfowl for nesting in tree cavities — ducklings leap from the nest hole to the ground on their first day. Nest boxes rescued it from near-extinction.
species.male: Iridescent green and purple crested head with intricate white face striping, chestnut breast, and a red eye.
species.female: Grey-brown with a distinctive white teardrop eye-ring on a grey face, and a white throat.
species.habitat: Wooded swamps, beaver ponds, and tree-lined rivers. One of very few ducks that can perch in trees.

species.name: Ring-necked Duck
species.region: Reported on 16.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Badly named — the chestnut neck ring is nearly invisible in the field, while the bold white bill ring is obvious. Prefers smaller, wooded ponds than most diving ducks.
species.male: Glossy black head with a peaked crown, black back, grey sides with a white spur at the shoulder, and a white-ringed grey bill.
species.female: Brown with a paler face, a white eye-ring, and the same distinctive bill pattern. Peaked head shape is diagnostic.
species.habitat: Wooded ponds, beaver ponds, marshes, and small lakes — smaller waters than most diving ducks use.

species.name: Blue-winged Teal
species.region: Reported on 14.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A long-distance migrant that arrives late in spring and leaves early in fall — some winter as far south as Peru. Travels in small, fast, low-flying flocks.
species.male: Slate-grey head with a bold white crescent in front of the eye, and a warm spotted brown body.
species.female: Mottled brown with a pale patch at the base of the bill. Both sexes flash a chalky blue forewing in flight.
species.habitat: Shallow marshes, prairie potholes, and pond edges with plenty of emergent vegetation.

species.name: Bufflehead
species.region: Reported on 14.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A tiny, buoyant diving duck that pops to the surface like a cork. Nests almost exclusively in old flicker cavities, which limits it to where big woodpeckers live.
species.male: Puffy black head with an enormous white wedge from eye to nape, glossed green and purple. Bright white body.
species.female: Dusky grey-brown with a small, neat white cheek patch. Much smaller and plainer than the male.
species.habitat: Small lakes, ponds, rivers, and sheltered coastal bays in winter.

species.name: Green Heron
species.region: Reported on 13.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: One of the very few tool-using birds — it drops feathers, twigs, or insects onto the water as bait and waits for fish to rise. Compact, secretive, and easy to miss until it barks its sharp alarm call.
species.male: Deep chestnut neck, dark iridescent green-blue back, a shaggy dark crown, and orange-yellow legs.
species.female: Very similar to the male, typically a little duller with less intense leg color outside breeding season.
species.habitat: Wooded swamps, stream edges, and pond margins with overhanging cover. Hunts from the water's edge.

species.name: American Herring Gull
species.region: Reported on 13.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The archetypal 'seagull' of the North Atlantic. Highly intelligent — known to drop clams onto rocks and pavement to crack them, and to stamp on wet sand to imitate rain and bring worms up.
species.male: White head and body, grey back, black wingtips with white spots, and a heavy yellow bill with a red spot near the tip.
species.female: Identical to the male. Takes four years to mature, passing through a confusing series of mottled brown plumages.
species.habitat: Coasts, harbors, large lakes, and landfills. Nests colonially on islands and rooftops.

species.name: Bonaparte's Gull
species.region: Reported on 12.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Lesser Scaup
species.region: Reported on 12.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The most abundant diving duck in North America, gathering in rafts that can number in the thousands. Told from Greater Scaup by a peaked, not rounded, crown.
species.male: Black head glossed purple, black breast, white sides, and a grey vermiculated back. Yellow eye.
species.female: Dark brown with a crisp white patch at the base of the bill. Yellow eye and peaked crown.
species.habitat: Large lakes, reservoirs, and coastal bays. Breeds on northern prairie and boreal wetlands.

species.name: Trumpeter Swan
species.region: Reported on 13.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: North America's largest native waterfowl and one of the heaviest flying birds on Earth. Hunted nearly to extinction, it has recovered through intensive reintroduction — a genuine conservation success.
species.male: All white with a black bill and black lores. Cobs are slightly larger than pens but otherwise identical.
species.female: Identical plumage. The pen incubates while the cob defends the territory. Both give the resonant trumpet call.
species.habitat: Large clear lakes, slow rivers, and marshes with abundant submerged plants. Needs a long runway of open water to take off.

species.name: Pied-billed Grebe
species.region: Reported on 11.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A stubby, secretive grebe that would rather sink slowly out of sight than fly. It can control its buoyancy by trapping or releasing air in its feathers, submerging like a submarine.
species.male: Brown overall with a black throat patch and a thick pale bill crossed by a black ring in breeding season.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both lose the bill ring and throat patch in winter, becoming plain buffy brown.
species.habitat: Ponds, marshes, and slow rivers with dense emergent vegetation to hide in.

species.name: Redhead
species.region: Reported on 11.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A diving duck that frequently lays its eggs in other ducks' nests, letting the host raise its young. Winters in huge rafts, especially on the Texas Gulf coast.
species.male: Rounded coppery-red head, black breast, and grey body — like a Canvasback but with a rounder head and a shorter blue-grey bill.
species.female: Warm brown overall with a slightly paler face and a dark-tipped grey bill. Rounded head shape is the key.
species.habitat: Marshes and prairie potholes for breeding; large lakes and coastal bays in winter.

species.name: Spotted Sandpiper
species.region: Reported on 11.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Teeters its rear end constantly, as if perpetually off balance. Unusually, the female is the aggressor — she arrives first, defends the territory, and may leave several males to raise her broods.
species.male: Bold dark spots on a white breast in summer; plain white below in winter. Constant bobbing motion and stiff, fluttery wingbeats.
species.female: Same spotted plumage but often more heavily marked than the male. She mates with several males in a season.
species.habitat: Edges of almost any freshwater — streams, ponds, lakeshores — and coastal shorelines in migration.

species.name: Gadwall
species.region: Reported on 10.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A subtly beautiful duck that rewards a second look — what seems plain grey at a distance is finely vermiculated up close. Often steals food from diving ducks as they surface.
species.male: Intricately patterned grey body, black rear end, and a small white speculum patch visible at rest.
species.female: Mottled brown, very like a small female Mallard, but with an orange-sided bill and that white wing patch.
species.habitat: Marshes, ponds, and reservoirs with plenty of submerged vegetation.

species.name: Hooded Merganser
species.region: Reported on 11.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A small, spectacular fish-eating duck with a fan-shaped crest it can raise and lower. Has a third eyelid that acts as built-in goggles for underwater hunting.
species.male: Black head with a huge white fan-shaped crest, chestnut flanks, and bold black-and-white breast stripes.
species.female: Grey-brown with a shaggy cinnamon crest. Subtle but unmistakable once you know the shape.
species.habitat: Wooded ponds, swamps, and slow rivers. Nests in tree cavities, often alongside Wood Ducks.

species.name: Lesser Yellowlegs
species.region: Reported on 10.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Northern Shoveler
species.region: Reported on 10.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Instantly told by its enormous spatulate bill, which it uses to strain tiny invertebrates from the water. Groups sometimes swim in tight circles to stir food to the surface.
species.male: Green head, white breast, chestnut flanks, and a huge black bill — a striking, almost cartoonish combination.
species.female: Mottled brown with the same oversized bill, which is orange-tinged along the edges.
species.habitat: Shallow, mucky wetlands and marshes rich in invertebrates. Also uses sewage lagoons and flooded fields.

species.name: Red-breasted Merganser
species.region: Reported on 11.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The saltwater merganser, wintering mostly on coasts and the Great Lakes. Holds the record for the fastest level flight of any duck, clocked over 130 km/h.
species.male: Shaggy double crest, dark green head, white neck ring, and a speckled rusty breast.
species.female: Grey-brown with a shaggy rufous head that blends gradually into the neck — no crisp border like the Common Merganser.
species.habitat: Coastal bays, estuaries, and the Great Lakes in winter. Breeds on northern lakes and tundra ponds.

species.name: American Coot
species.region: Reported on 10.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Not a duck at all but a rail, with lobed toes instead of webbed feet. Runs splashily across the water to take off, and squabbles constantly in winter flocks.
species.male: Slate-grey to black body with a bright white chicken-like bill and a red eye. Greenish lobed feet.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both sexes aggressively defend territory with charging, splashing displays.
species.habitat: Ponds, marshes, lakes, and reservoirs with open water and emergent vegetation. Often grazes on lawns nearby.

species.name: Caspian Tern
species.region: Reported on 9.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Least Sandpiper
species.region: Reported on 10.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: American Wigeon
species.region: Reported on 9.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A grazing duck as likely to be found on a wet lawn or golf course as on water. Gives a distinctive three-note whistle that carries across a marsh.
species.male: Green swoosh behind the eye on a grey head, with a creamy-white crown — hence the old name 'baldpate'. Pinkish-brown breast.
species.female: Warm grey-brown with a plain greyish head and a small blue-grey bill tipped in black.
species.habitat: Marshes, lakes, and flooded fields. Frequently grazes on shortgrass well away from water.

species.name: Common Loon
species.region: Reported on 8.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Common Merganser
species.region: Reported on 8.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A large, streamlined fish hunter with a serrated 'sawbill' for gripping slippery prey. Broods of ducklings often ride on the mother's back.
species.male: Clean white body, glossy dark green head with no crest, and a slender red bill.
species.female: Grey body with a sharply demarcated rusty-cinnamon head, a shaggy crest, and a crisp white chin.
species.habitat: Clear rivers, large lakes, and reservoirs. Prefers clean water where it can hunt fish by sight.

species.name: American White Pelican
species.region: Reported on 7.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: One of the largest birds in North America, with a wingspan approaching nine feet. Unlike Brown Pelicans it never plunge-dives — instead, groups herd fish cooperatively into the shallows and scoop them up together.
species.male: Enormous white bird with black flight feathers and a huge orange bill and pouch. Grows a strange horn on the bill when breeding.
species.female: Identical to the male, including the breeding bill horn, which is shed after eggs are laid.
species.habitat: Large lakes, reservoirs, and coastal lagoons. Breeds on isolated islands in prairie and western lakes.

species.name: Common Goldeneye
species.region: Reported on 7.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Nicknamed 'whistler' for the loud whirring hum its wings make in flight — audible long before the bird is seen. Winters on cold, open water, often the last duck to leave a freezing lake.
species.male: Glossy green-black head with a round white spot before the eye, bright white body, and a striking golden eye.
species.female: Chocolate-brown head with a grey body and a dark bill usually tipped in yellow. Golden eye.
species.habitat: Large lakes, rivers, and coastal bays. Breeds in tree cavities in northern forests.

species.name: Horned Grebe
species.region: Reported on 8.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Northern Pintail
species.region: Reported on 7.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: An elegant, long-necked dabbler and one of the earliest spring migrants. The drake's needle-thin tail streamers are unmistakable in flight.
species.male: Chocolate-brown head, a white breast stripe running up the neck, grey body, and long black central tail feathers.
species.female: Mottled buff-brown, plainer than most female ducks, with a slender neck and grey bill. Elegant even in drab plumage.
species.habitat: Shallow wetlands, flooded fields, and prairie potholes. Winters on coastal marsh and rice fields.

species.name: Ruddy Duck
species.region: Reported on 7.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A compact stiff-tailed diver with an outsized attitude. The courting male drums his blue bill against his chest fast enough to froth the water with bubbles.
species.male: Breeding: bright chestnut body, black cap, bold white cheek, and an electric sky-blue bill. Tail often cocked straight up.
species.female: Grey-brown with a dark cap and a single dark line crossing the pale cheek. Also cocks the stiff tail.
species.habitat: Marshes and prairie potholes for breeding; open lakes, reservoirs, and coastal bays in winter.

species.name: Sandhill Crane
species.region: Reported on 8.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Among the oldest surviving bird species on Earth — a fossil from Nebraska matches the modern bird and is about ten million years old. The bugling call carries for miles, and migrating flocks stage in the hundreds of thousands.
species.male: Tall grey body, bright red crown of bare skin, and a bustle of drooping feathers over the rump. Often stained rusty from preening with iron-rich mud.
species.female: Identical to the male. Pairs mate for life and perform elaborate leaping courtship dances together.
species.habitat: Wet meadows, marshes, and prairie for breeding; harvested grain fields and river roosts on migration.

species.name: Semipalmated Sandpiper
species.region: Reported on 8.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Solitary Sandpiper
species.region: Reported on 7.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: American Black Duck
species.region: Reported on 6.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A dark, wary duck of the eastern marshes. Declining, in part because it hybridizes readily with the expanding Mallard, blurring the species line.
species.male: Very dark sooty-brown body contrasting with a paler grey-brown head. Olive-yellow bill. Violet speculum without white borders.
species.female: Similar to the male but with a duller olive bill. In flight both sexes flash brilliant white wing linings against the dark body.
species.habitat: Salt marsh, coastal bays, wooded swamps, and beaver ponds. Prefers quieter, wilder water than Mallards.

species.name: Common Tern
species.region: Reported on 6.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Dunlin
species.region: Reported on 7.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Greater Yellowlegs
species.region: Reported on 6.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Green-winged Teal
species.region: Reported on 7.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The smallest dabbling duck on the continent — fast, agile, and given to twisting, shorebird-like flock flight.
species.male: Chestnut head with a sweeping iridescent green patch from the eye to the nape, a vertical white bar on the side, and a spotted breast.
species.female: Mottled brown and easy to overlook, but the tiny size and bright green speculum give her away.
species.habitat: Shallow marshes, mudflats, and flooded fields. Feeds in water barely deep enough to float in.

species.name: Canvasback
species.region: Reported on 6.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The aristocrat of diving ducks, with a long sloping forehead that makes the head and bill look like one continuous wedge. Flies fast and high in long lines.
species.male: Chestnut-red head, black breast, and a strikingly pale white-grey body that gleams at distance.
species.female: Soft brown head and breast with a pale grey body. The distinctive sloped head profile identifies her at any range.
species.habitat: Large lakes, reservoirs, and coastal bays in winter. Breeds on prairie marshes.

species.name: Pectoral Sandpiper
species.region: Reported on 6.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Semipalmated Plover
species.region: Reported on 6.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Common Gallinule
species.region: Reported on 5.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Black-necked Stilt
species.region: Reported on 3.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Great Black-backed Gull
species.region: Reported on 3.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Greater Scaup
species.region: Reported on 3.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Tundra Swan
species.region: Reported on 4.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The most numerous swan in North America, migrating in long lines between the Arctic and coastal wintering grounds. Smaller and shorter-necked than the Trumpeter, with a higher, yelping call.
species.male: All white with a black bill, usually showing a small yellow teardrop at the base in front of the eye.
species.female: Identical to the male, slightly smaller. Pairs mate for life and migrate as family groups.
species.habitat: Shallow lakes, estuaries, flooded fields, and coastal bays in winter. Breeds on Arctic tundra.

species.name: Snowy Egret
species.region: Reported on 2.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The 'golden slippers' bird — it shuffles its bright yellow feet through the mud to flush out prey, then snatches it. More active and animated than the larger egrets.
species.male: All white with a slender black bill, black legs, and vivid yellow feet. Wispy recurved plumes when breeding.
species.female: Identical to the male, including the yellow feet and delicate breeding plumes.
species.habitat: Salt marsh, mudflats, mangroves, and shallow freshwater wetlands.
