species.name: Snow Goose
species.region: Reported on 1.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Migrates in enormous, noisy flocks that can darken the sky. Populations have exploded to the point of damaging their Arctic breeding grounds. Comes in a white morph and a dark 'Blue Goose' morph.
species.male: White morph: all white with black wingtips and a pink bill with a dark 'grinning patch'. Blue morph: dark grey-brown body with a white head.
species.female: Identical to the male in both morphs, slightly smaller on average.
species.habitat: Marshes, wet grasslands, and agricultural fields on migration. Winters on coastal marsh and farmland.

species.name: Surf Scoter
species.region: Reported on 1.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: White-rumped Sandpiper
species.region: Reported on 1.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: White-winged Scoter
species.region: Reported on 0.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Willet
species.region: Reported on 0.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Wilson's Phalarope
species.region: Reported on 0.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Turkey Vulture
species.region: Reported on 42.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Finds carrion by smell — a genuinely rare ability among birds, and so acute that gas companies have used it to find pipeline leaks by adding the same compound vultures detect. Soars for hours without a wingbeat.
species.male: All dark brown-black with a bare red head and silvery flight feathers below. Holds wings in a shallow V and rocks side to side.
species.female: Identical to the male. Sexes cannot be told apart in the field.
species.habitat: Open country of every kind — farmland, roadsides, forest edge, and desert. Roosts communally in big trees.

species.name: Bald Eagle
species.region: Reported on 25.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Brought back from the edge by the DDT ban and the Endangered Species Act — from a few hundred pairs in the lower 48 to tens of thousands today. The white head and tail don't appear until about year five.
species.male: Unmistakable: white head and tail, dark brown body, and a massive yellow bill.
species.female: Identical in plumage but distinctly larger — the most pronounced size difference of any North American raptor.
species.habitat: Always near big water — coasts, large rivers, and reservoirs. Nests in huge trees with a commanding view.

species.name: Red-tailed Hawk
species.region: Reported on 16.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The default large hawk of North America, and the one on every highway light pole. Its piercing scream is so iconic that Hollywood dubs it over footage of Bald Eagles, which actually sound rather weak.
species.male: Brown above, pale below with a dark belly band, and a brick-red tail. Extremely variable across its range.
species.female: Identical plumage, larger and heavier. The red tail is diagnostic in adults of both sexes.
species.habitat: Almost anywhere with an open hunting field and a high perch — farmland, roadsides, desert, and city parks.

species.name: Osprey
species.region: Reported on 13.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A fish specialist found on every continent but Antarctica. It can reverse an outer toe to grip a fish with two toes front and two back, and its footpads are studded with spines for holding slippery prey.
species.male: Dark brown above, white below, with a white head crossed by a broad dark eye stripe. Wings crooked at the wrist in flight.
species.female: Very similar, but usually with a heavier brown 'necklace' of streaking across the breast. Larger than the male.
species.habitat: Any large water with fish — coasts, lakes, rivers, reservoirs. Nests on platforms, poles, and dead trees.

species.name: Red-shouldered Hawk
species.region: Reported on 10.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A loud, dramatic hawk of wet woodlands. Its ringing 'kee-aah' is one of the most frequently mimicked calls in the forest — Blue Jays imitate it constantly, apparently to clear feeders.
species.male: Rufous-barred underparts, rusty shoulder patches, and a boldly black-and-white banded tail. Pale crescent 'windows' near the wingtips in flight.
species.female: Identical plumage, larger. The translucent wing crescents are the best flight mark for both sexes.
species.habitat: Wet bottomland forest, riparian woods, and swamps. Also mature suburbs with big trees.

species.name: Cooper's Hawk
species.region: Reported on 8.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The classic backyard bird-hawk. It has adapted so well to suburbs that urban Cooper's Hawks often survive better than forest ones — the feeders are, from the hawk's view, a buffet.
species.male: Blue-grey above, rusty-barred below, with a dark cap contrasting with a paler nape, and a long rounded tail.
species.female: Same pattern, browner-backed, and substantially larger — approaching crow size.
species.habitat: Mature woodland, woodland edge, parks, and leafy suburbs. Hunts from concealed perches.

species.name: American Kestrel
species.region: Reported on 6.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The smallest and most colorful falcon in North America, and in worrying decline. It can see ultraviolet light, which makes the urine trails of voles glow like runway lights.
species.male: Rusty back and tail, blue-grey wings, a rufous crown, and two bold black 'sideburn' stripes on a white face.
species.female: Rusty-brown wings as well as back, all finely barred, with streaked underparts. Same facial pattern.
species.habitat: Open country — farmland, roadsides, meadows, and deserts. Nests in cavities; readily uses nest boxes.

species.name: Eastern Screech-Owl
species.region: Reported on 5.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A tiny owl hiding in plain sight across the East — often roosting in a hole in a suburban shade tree. Comes in a grey morph and a rufous morph, sometimes in the same brood.
species.male: Small, stocky, with prominent ear tufts and yellow eyes. Bark-patterned grey or rusty red plumage.
species.female: Identical plumage, slightly larger. The whinnying, descending call is unmistakable at night.
species.habitat: Woodlands, orchards, parks, and leafy neighborhoods. Nests in cavities and readily uses nest boxes.

species.name: Northern Harrier
species.region: Reported on 5.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Hunts by ear as much as by eye — it has an owl-like facial disc that funnels sound, letting it find voles in thick grass. Quarters low over fields with wings held in a lazy V.
species.male: Pale grey above, white below, with black wingtips — the 'grey ghost'. Bold white rump patch.
species.female: Brown above and streaky buff below, much bigger and browner than the male. Same white rump.
species.habitat: Marshes, wet meadows, grassland, and farm fields. Nests on the ground in dense cover.

species.name: Black Vulture
species.region: Reported on 3.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Has a poor sense of smell, so it watches Turkey Vultures and follows them down to a carcass — then bullies them off it. Expanding steadily northward.
species.male: All black with a bare grey wrinkled head, short square tail, and white patches at the wingtips. Flaps often, in quick shallow beats.
species.female: Identical to the male. Highly social; roosts and forages in family groups.
species.habitat: Open and semi-open country, especially near towns, landfills, and highways.

species.name: Barred Owl
species.region: Reported on 2.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The 'who-cooks-for-you' owl, and the most vocal of the large owls. Has expanded west in the last century, where it now outcompetes and displaces the endangered Spotted Owl.
species.male: Mottled grey-brown with horizontal barring on the chest and vertical streaks on the belly. Dark eyes and no ear tufts.
species.female: Identical to the male, slightly larger. Both call year-round, often in raucous duets.
species.habitat: Mature moist forest, wooded swamps, and river bottomland. Also large forested suburbs.

species.name: Broad-winged Hawk
species.region: Reported on 3.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A small woodland buteo that migrates in spectacular swirling flocks called kettles — tens of thousands can pass a hawk-watch site in a single September day, bound for South America.
species.male: Compact brown hawk with rusty barring on the breast and a tail crossed by broad black-and-white bands.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both are far more often heard — a thin, plaintive whistle — than seen in summer.
species.habitat: Interior deciduous and mixed forest. Nests in dense woods, hunts along openings and roads.

species.name: Rough-legged Hawk
species.region: Reported on 1.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Sharp-shinned Hawk
species.region: Reported on 1.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A tiny, hyper-agile forest hawk built to chase songbirds through branches. The smallest accipiter in North America, and a familiar terror at winter feeders.
species.male: Blue-grey above, finely rusty-barred below, with a small head, thin legs, and a square-tipped tail. Red eye when adult.
species.female: Same pattern but noticeably larger — a big female approaches a small male Cooper's Hawk in size.
species.habitat: Dense forest and woodland edge; increasingly suburban yards with feeders in winter.

species.name: Short-eared Owl
species.region: Reported on 1.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Great Horned Owl
species.region: Reported on 1.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The continent's most powerful and adaptable owl, with a grip strength of over 300 psi. It's one of the very few predators that regularly kills skunks — owls have a poor sense of smell.
species.male: Big, barrel-shaped, mottled grey-brown with prominent ear tufts, a white throat bib, and huge yellow eyes.
species.female: Identical in plumage but larger. She has the deeper body but he has the deeper hoot.
species.habitat: Nearly every habitat on the continent — forest, desert, swamp, farmland, and city park.

species.name: Merlin
species.region: Reported on 1.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Northern Saw-whet Owl
species.region: Reported on 0.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Peregrine Falcon
species.region: Reported on 1.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The fastest animal on the planet — a stooping Peregrine has been clocked over 240 mph. Wiped out east of the Rockies by DDT, then restored partly by releasing birds onto city skyscrapers, which serve nicely as cliffs.
species.male: Slate-grey above, barred white below, with a heavy black 'helmet' and moustache mark. Long pointed wings.
species.female: Identical plumage, up to a third larger than the male.
species.habitat: Cliffs, coasts, and city skyscrapers. Hunts over open ground, water, and downtown streets alike.

species.name: Snowy Owl
species.region: Reported on 1.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
species.region: Reported on 50.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Badly named — the red belly is a faint pink wash you'll almost never see, while the flaming red cap is obvious. It has expanded steadily northward over the last century and is now a feeder staple.
species.male: Boldly black-and-white barred back with red running from the bill all the way over the crown to the nape.
species.female: Same zebra back, but red only on the nape — the crown is grey. This is the easiest way to sex them.
species.habitat: Deciduous woods, parks, and wooded suburbs. Very common at suet and sunflower feeders.

species.name: Downy Woodpecker
species.region: Reported on 44.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The smallest woodpecker in North America and by far the most familiar. Its short bill lets it forage on weed stems and thin twigs where bigger woodpeckers can't go.
species.male: Black and white with a white back stripe, a small stubby bill, and a red patch on the back of the head.
species.female: Identical but with no red at all on the head. Otherwise inseparable from the male.
species.habitat: Woods, parks, orchards, and suburbs with trees. A cornerstone of winter mixed flocks with chickadees.

species.name: Northern Flicker
species.region: Reported on 35.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The woodpecker that feeds on the ground, hammering into ant colonies with a barbed, sticky tongue that extends two inches past the bill. Eastern birds flash gold under the wings; western birds flash salmon-red.
species.male: Brown with black barring, a black bib, a spotted belly, and a black moustache stripe. Red nape crescent in the East.
species.female: Identical, but with no moustache stripe — the cleanest sex mark of any North American woodpecker.
species.habitat: Open woodland, parks, and suburbs. Very often seen probing lawns and bare ground for ants.

species.name: Hairy Woodpecker
species.region: Reported on 15.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Essentially a Downy Woodpecker scaled up, and one of the great beginner ID challenges. The giveaway is the bill: on a Hairy it's nearly as long as the head is wide; on a Downy it's a stub.
species.male: Black and white with a white back stripe and a long, chisel-like bill. Red patch on the back of the head.
species.female: Same pattern, no red on the head. Size and bill length separate her from a female Downy.
species.habitat: Mature forest of all kinds, from boreal to montane to eastern hardwood. Prefers larger trees than Downy.

species.name: Red-headed Woodpecker
species.region: Reported on 13.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: One of only four woodpeckers that stores food, and the only one that covers the cache with wood or bark. Declining sharply as dead trees are cleared and oak savanna disappears.
species.male: Unmistakable: an entirely crimson head, snow-white underparts, and black wings with huge white patches.
species.female: Identical to the male. Juveniles have brown heads and are often mistaken for a different species.
species.habitat: Open oak woodland, savanna, dead timber in swamps, and roadside snags. Needs standing dead wood.

species.name: Pileated Woodpecker
species.region: Reported on 13.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Crow-sized, and the largest woodpecker in North America now that the Ivory-billed is almost certainly gone. It chisels distinctive rectangular holes so deep they can snap small trees in half.
species.male: Huge black bird with a flaming red crest, a white neck stripe, and a red moustache stripe.
species.female: Same, but the forecrown is grey-black rather than red, and she has no red moustache.
species.habitat: Mature forest with large dead trees. Needs big timber, but tolerates wooded suburbs with old trees.

species.name: Yellow-bellied Sapsucker
species.region: Reported on 8.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: American Robin
species.region: Reported on 75.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Everyone's first bird. It doesn't actually leave in winter so much as change behavior — flocking into woods to eat fruit, then returning to lawns when the ground thaws, which is why it seems to 'arrive' each spring.
species.male: Dark grey-black head and back, brick-orange breast, white eye-arcs, and a yellow bill.
species.female: Paler and browner overall, with a duller orange breast and a greyer head. Obvious when a pair is side by side.
species.habitat: Lawns, parks, gardens, forests, and tundra alike — one of the most adaptable birds on the continent.

species.name: Northern Cardinal
species.region: Reported on 72.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The state bird of seven states, more than any other. Unusually, the female sings too — often from the nest, apparently telling the male when to bring food. The male's red comes entirely from carotenoids in his diet.
species.male: Brilliant scarlet all over, with a black face mask and throat, a tall crest, and a heavy orange-red bill.
species.female: Warm buff-brown with red edging on the crest, wings and tail, and the same orange bill and dark face.
species.habitat: Dense shrubs, woodland edge, thickets, and gardens. Perhaps the most beloved feeder bird in America.

species.name: Blue Jay
species.region: Reported on 71.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Mimics hawk calls convincingly, apparently to scatter birds from a feeder before dropping in. Also one of the great foresters — a single jay may cache thousands of acorns a year, and the ones it forgets become oak trees.
species.male: Brilliant blue above with a black necklace, white wingbars and tail corners, and a bold crest. The blue is structural, not pigment.
species.female: Identical to the male. Sexes cannot be reliably told apart in the field.
species.habitat: Woodlands, parks, and suburbs with oaks. Bold, loud, and a fixture at feeders.

species.name: Red-winged Blackbird
species.region: Reported on 66.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Among the most abundant birds in North America. Males are ferociously territorial and will attack anything near the nest — horses, dogs, hawks, and joggers included. The liquid 'conk-la-REE!' is the sound of spring marshes.
species.male: Glossy black with brilliant red-and-yellow shoulder epaulettes, flared in display and hidden when not.
species.female: Heavily streaked dark brown and buff with a pale eyebrow — looks like a large, dark sparrow.
species.habitat: Cattail marshes, wet fields, and roadside ditches. Also farm fields and pastures.

species.name: American Goldfinch
species.region: Reported on 63.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The strictest vegetarian of any North American songbird — even the nestlings get almost no insects. It nests in late summer, timed to the thistle seed crop, long after every other songbird is done.
species.male: Summer: brilliant canary-yellow with a black cap, black wings, and white wingbars. Winter: drab olive-buff.
species.female: Duller yellow-olive with no black cap; in winter, plain buffy-grey.
species.habitat: Weedy fields, roadsides, gardens, and woodland edge. Abundant at nyjer feeders.

species.name: Song Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 56.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: One of the most variable birds in North America, with over 50 named subspecies — pale and small in the desert, huge and dark in the Aleutians. Often the first bird singing on a warm February morning.
species.male: Streaky brown above and below, with the breast streaks converging into a bold central spot. Rusty crown stripes.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both are best identified by the central breast spot and the pumping tail in flight.
species.habitat: Brushy areas near water — marsh edges, streamsides, thickets, hedgerows, and gardens.

species.name: Gray Catbird
species.region: Reported on 53.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Named for its cat-like mew. A gifted mimic and improviser — unlike a mockingbird it rarely repeats a phrase, instead stringing together a long, rambling, ever-changing performance from deep inside a thicket.
species.male: Uniform slate-grey with a black cap and a chestnut patch beneath the tail.
species.female: Identical to the male. Neither sex can be told apart in the field.
species.habitat: Dense thickets, hedgerows, and shrubby gardens. Skulking, but responds readily to pishing.

species.name: Mourning Dove
species.region: Reported on 53.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: One of the most abundant birds on the continent, with a population over 300 million. The mournful cooing is often mistaken for an owl. Can fly 55 mph, and the wings whistle sharply on takeoff.
species.male: Slender and long-tailed, soft pinkish-buff, with black spots on the wings and a small round head.
species.female: Nearly identical, marginally duller and browner with less neck iridescence.
species.habitat: Open and semi-open country of every kind — farms, suburbs, parks, roadsides, and desert.

species.name: Northern Yellow Warbler
species.region: Reported on 52.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: When a cowbird lays an egg in its nest, the Yellow Warbler simply builds a new floor over it and starts again — nests six stories tall have been found, each layer entombing a cowbird egg.
species.male: Brilliant egg-yolk yellow all over, with rusty-red streaks down the breast and large dark eyes on a plain face.
species.female: Plain yellow with no streaking and a slightly greenish wash above.
species.habitat: Willow and alder thickets along streams, wet shrubby areas, and gardens with dense shrubs.

species.name: Baltimore Oriole
species.region: Reported on 49.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Weaves a remarkable hanging pouch nest from plant fibers and string, suspended from the tip of a high branch. Comes readily to orange halves and grape jelly — one of the few birds you can reliably lure with fruit.
species.male: Flaming orange body with a solid black hood, black wings with a white bar, and an orange-and-black tail.
species.female: Yellow-orange below and olive-brown above, with two white wingbars. Older females can be quite orange.
species.habitat: Open deciduous woodland, riverside trees, parks, and leafy suburbs — especially tall elms and cottonwoods.

species.name: Tree Swallow
species.region: Reported on 47.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Unlike other swallows it can digest berries, especially bayberry, which lets it winter further north than any of its relatives and arrive back earliest in spring.
species.male: Iridescent blue-green above, clean white below, with a sharp line through the eye. Dazzling in sunlight.
species.female: Similar but often duller and browner — first-year females can be almost entirely brown above.
species.habitat: Open fields, marshes, and lakeshores. A cavity nester that took eagerly to bluebird boxes.

species.name: Common Grackle
species.region: Reported on 45.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Overlooked because it's everywhere, but a genuinely striking bird in good light. It soaks hard food in water to soften it, and will raid other birds' nests. Winter roosts can number in the millions.
species.male: Iridescent bronze body with a glossy blue-purple head, pale yellow eyes, and a long keel-shaped tail.
species.female: Duller and browner with less iridescence and a shorter, less keeled tail.
species.habitat: Open woods, lawns, parks, farm fields, and marshes. Roosts communally in enormous flocks.

species.name: Brown-headed Cowbird
species.region: Reported on 44.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: North America's most notorious brood parasite, laying its eggs in the nests of over 220 species. It followed bison herds historically, which is why it never settled long enough to raise its own young. Forest fragmentation has let it spread devastatingly.
species.male: Glossy green-black body with a distinctly brown head and a stout conical bill.
species.female: Uniform dull grey-brown with faint streaking and a pale throat — remarkably featureless.
species.habitat: Woodland edge, farmland, pastures, and suburbs. Follows livestock as it once followed bison.

species.name: Tufted Titmouse
species.region: Reported on 44.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Plucks hair directly from living animals to line its nest — squirrels, dogs, and occasionally unsuspecting humans. Its clear whistled 'PETER-peter-peter' is one of the definitive sounds of eastern woods.
species.male: Soft grey above, white below with rusty flanks, a pointed grey crest, and a black patch above the bill.
species.female: Identical to the male. Young often stay with the parents through the winter.
species.habitat: Deciduous woodland, parks, and suburbs with mature trees. Dominant at feeders.

species.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
species.region: Reported on 42.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The upside-down bird — it's the only group that routinely walks head-first down tree trunks, which lets it spot insects that woodpeckers moving upward miss entirely. Wedges nuts into bark and hammers them open, hence 'nut-hatch'.
species.male: Blue-grey above, white face and breast, a black cap, and rusty undertail. Stubby tail, long bill.
species.female: Very similar, but the cap is grey rather than glossy black — visible when a pair is together.
species.habitat: Mature deciduous and mixed forest, parks, and wooded suburbs. Joins chickadee flocks in winter.

species.name: American Crow
species.region: Reported on 41.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Recognizes individual human faces, holds grudges, and teaches those grudges to its offspring. Uses tools, solves multi-step puzzles, and gathers in winter roosts that can exceed a hundred thousand birds.
species.male: Entirely glossy black with a heavy bill and a fan-shaped tail. Iridescent purple sheen in good light.
species.female: Identical to the male. Young from previous years often stay to help raise the next brood.
diet: Almost anything — insects, grain, carrion, eggs, refuse
species.habitat: Extraordinarily adaptable. Farmland, city streets, parks, beaches, and landfills. Avoids only deep forest.

species.name: Dark-eyed Junco
species.region: Reported on 41.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The 'snowbird' that shows up under feeders the week the weather turns. Comes in strikingly different regional forms — slate-grey in the East, boldly black-and-rust in the Rockies — that were once considered separate species.
species.male: Eastern form: slate-grey hood, back and breast with a clean white belly, a pink bill, and white outer tail feathers that flash in flight.
species.female: Similar but browner and paler grey, with the same pink bill and white tail flashes.
species.habitat: Coniferous and mixed forest in summer; feeders, thickets, and woodland edge in winter.

species.name: European Starling
species.region: Reported on 40.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: All 200 million North American starlings descend from about 100 birds released in Central Park in the 1890s. A superb mimic, an aggressive cavity competitor that displaces native birds, and the author of the astonishing swirling murmurations of winter.
species.male: Winter: blackish and covered in white spots, with a dark bill. Summer: glossy iridescent green-purple with a bright yellow bill.
species.female: Same seasonal changes, slightly less glossy. In breeding condition his bill base is blue, hers is pink.
species.habitat: Cities, farms, suburbs, and fields. Nests in any cavity in any structure.

species.name: House Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 40.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Introduced to Brooklyn in 1851 and now among the most widespread birds on Earth. It has lived alongside humans since the dawn of agriculture, and is oddly now declining in many cities for reasons nobody fully understands.
species.male: Grey crown, chestnut nape, black bib, white cheek, and a streaked brown back. Bib size signals dominance.
species.female: Plain dusty brown, streaked above, with a buff eyebrow behind the eye and no bib at all.
species.habitat: Cities, suburbs, farms, and feedlots. Almost never found away from human settlement.

species.name: Common Yellowthroat
species.region: Reported on 38.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The little masked bandit of the marsh, and one of the most widespread warblers on the continent. Its rolling 'witchety-witchety-witchety' comes from deep in the cattails, and the singer is maddeningly hard to actually see.
species.male: Olive above with a bright yellow throat and a bold black bandit's mask edged in white above.
species.female: Olive with a yellow throat and no mask at all — plainer, and easy to puzzle over.
species.habitat: Marshes, wet thickets, and any dense low vegetation near water. Also drier overgrown fields.

species.name: White-throated Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 38.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Whistles a clear, plaintive 'Oh-sweet-Canada-Canada' that is the sound of the northern woods. Comes in two permanent morphs — white-striped and tan-striped — and, oddly, each almost always mates with the opposite type.
species.male: Bold black-and-white striped crown, a crisp white throat patch, and a bright yellow spot between eye and bill.
species.female: Same pattern; the tan-striped morph is duller in both sexes, so morph matters more than sex.
species.habitat: Forest understory, thickets, and brushy edges. Very common under winter feeders in the East.

species.name: Barn Swallow
species.region: Reported on 37.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The most widespread swallow on Earth, and one of the most thoroughly tied to us — it has almost entirely abandoned caves and cliffs in favor of barns, bridges, and culverts.
species.male: Deep steel-blue above, rusty-orange throat, buff underparts, and a long deeply forked tail with white spots.
species.female: Same pattern but with shorter tail streamers and paler underparts. Males with the longest tails get the best mates.
species.habitat: Open country near water — farms, fields, and marshes. Nests almost exclusively on human structures.

species.name: Northern House Wren
species.region: Reported on 38.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Tiny, brown, and utterly ferocious — it will pierce the eggs of any bird nesting nearby, including other wrens, to clear the neighborhood of competitors. Males build several dummy nests for the female to choose from.
species.male: Small, plain grey-brown with fine barring on the wings and tail, a faint eyebrow, and a frequently cocked tail.
species.female: Identical to the male. She picks one of his dummy nests and finishes it herself.
species.habitat: Thickets, brushy edges, gardens, and backyards. Uses nest boxes, old boots, mailboxes — almost any cavity.

species.name: Eastern Wood-Pewee
species.region: Reported on 37.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: House Finch
species.region: Reported on 37.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A western bird, illegally sold in New York as the 'Hollywood Finch' and released around 1940 when the law caught up. Those few birds seeded the entire eastern population, which has since met the westward-spreading originals.
species.male: Rosy-red head, breast and rump with a brown streaked back. Red intensity depends on diet — redder males are healthier.
species.female: Plain grey-brown with blurry streaking below and no distinct face pattern at all.
species.habitat: Towns, suburbs, farms, and parks. Nests in shrubs, hanging baskets, and building crevices.

species.name: Red-eyed Vireo
species.region: Reported on 37.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Nicknamed 'the preacher bird' — a single male can deliver over 20,000 song phrases in one day, still going in the noon heat when every other bird has gone quiet. One of the most abundant birds in eastern forests.
species.male: Olive-green above, white below, with a grey crown, a bold white eyebrow bordered in black, and a red eye.
species.female: Identical to the male. The red iris looks dark in poor light and takes a year to develop.
species.habitat: Deciduous forest canopy and woodland edge. Forages methodically through the leaves, high up.

species.name: Yellow-rumped Warbler
species.region: Reported on 37.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Nicknamed 'butter-butt'. Uniquely among warblers it can digest the waxes in bayberry and wax myrtle, which lets it winter far north of every other warbler — often the only one you'll see in a northern January.
species.male: Blue-grey with black streaking, a yellow crown, yellow side patches, and a bright yellow rump. Eastern birds have a white throat.
species.female: Browner and duller but with the same four yellow patches — crown, sides, and rump.
species.habitat: Conifer forest in summer; almost any woodland, thicket, or coastal scrub in winter.

species.name: Carolina Wren
species.region: Reported on 36.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: One of the loudest birds in North America for its size — the song carries half a mile. Pairs stay together year-round and duet. Hard winters with deep snow can crash the population overnight.
species.male: Rich rufous above, warm buff below, with a bold white eyebrow and a cocked tail.
species.female: Identical to the male, though only he sings the full ringing 'tea-kettle tea-kettle' song.
species.habitat: Brushy tangles, woodland understory, brush piles, and shrubby gardens. Nests in almost any cavity or crevice.

species.name: Eastern Warbling Vireo
species.region: Reported on 36.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: About as plain a bird as exists in North America — and then it opens its mouth and delivers a rich, rolling warble. Birders learn it by voice and give up on the plumage.
species.male: Drab grey-olive above, whitish below, with a pale eyebrow and no wingbars. Genuinely featureless.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both sexes sing, which is unusual among vireos.
species.habitat: Deciduous woodland, especially cottonwoods and willows along rivers, plus parks and orchards.

species.name: American Redstart
species.region: Reported on 34.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Flashes its brightly patterned tail open and shut to startle insects into flight, then snatches them mid-air. Constantly in motion, pivoting and fanning — it never seems to sit still for a second.
species.male: Glossy black with brilliant orange patches on the wings, tail, and sides. Unmistakable.
species.female: Grey and olive with the same pattern picked out in lemon-yellow instead of orange.
species.habitat: Deciduous second-growth woodland and thickets, especially near water.

species.name: Blue-gray Gnatcatcher
species.region: Reported on 33.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Builds an exquisite nest of plant down bound with spider silk and shingled with lichen, so well camouflaged it looks like a knot on the branch. Never stops moving or flicking its long tail.
species.male: Tiny, slim, blue-grey above and white below, with a white eye-ring and a long black tail edged in white. Thin black eyebrow when breeding.
species.female: Similar but greyer and lacking the black eyebrow.
species.habitat: Open deciduous woodland, oak scrub, and woodland edges.

species.name: Chipping Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 34.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A trim, tidy little sparrow of lawns and park edges. It historically lined its nest with horsehair, and now makes do with pet fur, string, and whatever fine fibers it can find.
species.male: Bright rufous cap, a crisp white eyebrow over a black eye-line, and a clean grey unstreaked breast.
species.female: Identical to the male. In winter the cap dulls to streaky brown and the bird becomes far less distinctive.
species.habitat: Open woodland, parks, lawns, and suburbs — especially short grass with trees nearby.

species.name: Indigo Bunting
species.region: Reported on 30.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: There is no blue pigment in the feathers at all — the color is a trick of light scattering, so in shade the bird looks flat black. Migrates at night, navigating by the stars, which it learns as a nestling.
species.male: Dazzling electric blue all over in summer; brown with blue flecks in winter. Heavy conical bill.
species.female: Plain warm brown with faint breast streaking and a whitish throat.
species.habitat: Brushy field edges, roadsides, and second growth. Sings from exposed high perches all summer long.

species.name: Carolina Chickadee
species.region: Reported on 28.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The southern counterpart to the Black-capped, nearly identical to look at. Where their ranges meet they hybridize along a narrow band that has been creeping north with warming winters.
species.male: Black cap and bib with white cheeks — like a Black-capped but slightly smaller, with a crisper bib edge and less white in the wing.
species.female: Identical to the male. Best separated from Black-capped by its faster four-note song.
species.habitat: Deciduous woods, swamps, parks, and suburbs across the Southeast.

species.name: Cedar Waxwing
species.region: Reported on 27.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The most elegant bird at any feeder-less winter berry tree. Flocks pass berries down a line from bird to bird. They occasionally get drunk on fermented fruit and fly into windows.
species.male: Silky fawn-grey with a black mask, a jaunty crest, a yellow-tipped tail, and red waxy droplets on the wing.
species.female: Nearly identical. The red wing tips increase with age rather than differing by sex.
species.habitat: Fruiting trees anywhere — woods, parks, orchards, and suburbs. Highly nomadic, following the fruit crop.

species.name: Eastern Towhee
species.region: Reported on 27.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A huge, dramatically marked sparrow that forages by kicking backward with both feet at once — a distinctive double-scratch that rustles loudly in the leaf litter. Sings 'drink-your-TEA!'
species.male: Black hood, back and tail, with bright rufous sides and a white belly. Staring red eye.
species.female: Identical pattern, but with warm brown replacing all the black — much more cryptic.
species.habitat: Dense shrubby undergrowth, thickets, and woodland edge with heavy leaf litter.

species.name: Ruby-throated Hummingbird
species.region: Reported on 27.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The only hummingbird that breeds east of the Great Plains. It crosses 500 miles of open Gulf of Mexico in a single flight, powered entirely by fat it stored beforehand — doubling its body weight to do it.
species.male: Iridescent emerald above with a brilliant ruby gorget that flashes or looks black depending on the light.
species.female: Green above, white below, with a plain throat and white-tipped outer tail feathers. Slightly larger than the male.
species.habitat: Gardens, woodland edge, and parks with tubular flowers. Readily comes to sugar-water feeders.

species.name: Eastern Kingbird
species.region: Reported on 26.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The scientific name means 'tyrant of tyrants' and it earns it — a kingbird will ride a hawk's back, pecking, until it leaves. On the Amazon wintering grounds it becomes a peaceable fruit-eater in flocks.
species.male: Slate-black above, white below, with a white terminal band across the tail like a dipped paintbrush.
species.female: Identical to the male. The concealed red crown stripe is almost never seen.
species.habitat: Open fields, orchards, and pond edges with scattered perches — fences, wires, and dead branches.

species.name: Palm Warbler
species.region: Reported on 26.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Chimney Swift
species.region: Reported on 25.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A 'flying cigar' that spends virtually its whole life airborne — it cannot perch, only cling to vertical walls. Autumn roosts of thousands funnel into a single chimney at dusk, a genuine spectacle.
species.male: Uniform sooty grey-brown with a slightly paler throat, long curved wings, and a stubby tail. Chattering, twittering calls.
species.female: Identical to the male; the sexes can't be separated in the field.
species.habitat: The open sky above almost anything. Nests and roosts inside chimneys, air shafts, and hollow trees.

species.name: Magnolia Warbler
species.region: Reported on 24.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Northern Parula
species.region: Reported on 23.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Rose-breasted Grosbeak
species.region: Reported on 24.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The male sings a rich, liquid warble — like a robin that took singing lessons — and unusually, he sings while sitting on the eggs. His arrival at feeders each May is a highlight of the eastern spring.
species.male: Black head and back, white underparts, and a bold triangular blood-red bib. Massive pale bill.
species.female: Heavily brown-streaked with a bold white eyebrow and two white wingbars — often mistaken for a big sparrow.
species.habitat: Mature deciduous forest and forest edge. A frequent spring feeder visitor.

species.name: Great Crested Flycatcher
species.region: Reported on 23.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A cavity-nesting flycatcher with a strange habit: it lines the nest with shed snakeskin, or failing that, cellophane and plastic wrappers. The reason is still argued over — perhaps to deter predators.
species.male: Grey throat, bright lemon-yellow belly, rufous tail and wing panels, and a shaggy crest.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both are far more often heard — a loud rising 'WHEEP!' — than seen in the canopy.
species.habitat: Mature deciduous forest and wooded suburbs. Forages high; nests in natural cavities and nest boxes.

species.name: Ruby-crowned Kinglet
species.region: Reported on 23.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A frantic scrap of a bird, never still, and possessed of a song wildly out of proportion to its size — loud, rich, and tumbling. The male's red crown is hidden until he gets excited, then it flares like a struck match.
species.male: Drab olive with two white wingbars, a broken white eye-ring, and a scarlet crown patch usually kept concealed.
species.female: Identical but with no red crown at all. Both flick their wings constantly.
species.habitat: Conifers in summer; nearly any woodland, thicket, or garden on migration and in winter.

species.name: Black-capped Chickadee
species.region: Reported on 22.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Every autumn it grows new brain cells in the hippocampus to store the locations of thousands of hidden seeds, then shrinks it back in spring. The 'chick-a-dee' call encodes threat level — more 'dee's means a more dangerous predator.
species.male: Black cap and bib, crisp white cheeks, grey back, and buff-washed flanks.
species.female: Identical to the male. The whistled 'fee-bee' song is given mostly by males.
species.habitat: Woodlands, thickets, parks, and wooded suburbs. The nucleus of winter mixed-species flocks.

species.name: Tennessee Warbler
species.region: Reported on 21.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Eastern Phoebe
species.region: Reported on 21.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: One of the earliest migrants to return each spring, and the subject of the first bird-banding experiment in North America — Audubon tied silver thread to nestlings' legs in 1804 to prove they came back.
species.male: Plain grey-brown above, whitish below, with a dark head and no eye-ring or wingbars. Wags its tail constantly.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both are best told by the tail-wag and the raspy 'FEE-bee' call.
species.habitat: Woodland edges near water. Nests under bridges, eaves, and culverts — almost always on a man-made structure.

species.name: Golden-crowned Kinglet
species.region: Reported on 20.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Weighs about as much as two pennies yet survives nights of −40°F in boreal forest, huddling together and shivering all night long. Constantly flicking its wings as it forages.
species.male: Tiny olive-grey bird with a black-bordered crown stripe that is flaming orange in the center.
species.female: Identical but the crown stripe is pure lemon-yellow with no orange.
species.habitat: Coniferous forest, especially spruce and fir. Spreads into mixed woods and suburbs in winter.

species.name: Swainson's Thrush
species.region: Reported on 21.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Bay-breasted Warbler
species.region: Reported on 20.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Chestnut-sided Warbler
species.region: Reported on 19.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Eastern Bluebird
species.region: Reported on 20.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Nearly lost to competition from introduced starlings and House Sparrows, then rescued by an army of volunteers who put up hundreds of thousands of nest boxes along 'bluebird trails'. One of conservation's quiet triumphs.
species.male: Vivid royal blue above with a rich rusty-orange throat and breast and a white belly.
species.female: Softer grey-blue above with a paler orange breast and a white eye-ring. Blue shows brightest in the wings and tail.
species.habitat: Open country with scattered trees — pastures, orchards, golf courses, and roadsides. Depends on nest boxes.

species.name: Field Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 19.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Its song is a series of sweet, sad whistles that accelerate into a trill — often compared to a ping-pong ball dropped on a table. Disappearing as old fields are developed or replanted.
species.male: Rusty cap, a plain grey face with a rufous eye-line, a white eye-ring, and a distinctive bright pink bill.
species.female: Identical to the male. The pink bill is the giveaway for both sexes.
species.habitat: Overgrown fields, brushy pastures, and young second growth. Needs shrubby early-successional habitat.

species.name: Nashville Warbler
species.region: Reported on 20.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Wood Thrush
species.region: Reported on 20.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Its ethereal, fluting 'ee-oh-lay' is the signature sound of the eastern summer forest. Populations have fallen by more than half since 1970, hit from both ends by forest fragmentation here and deforestation in Central America.
species.male: Warm rusty-brown head and back fading to olive, with a white breast boldly marked in large black spots.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both sing, but only the male gives the full song.
species.habitat: Interior deciduous forest with a moist shaded understory. Needs large unbroken woods.

species.name: Black-throated Green Warbler
species.region: Reported on 19.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Sings a drowsy, buzzy 'zee-zee-zee-zoo-zee' that carries through the summer canopy. A male may sing it over 400 times an hour, which is a remarkable amount of enthusiasm for a bird that weighs a third of an ounce.
species.male: Bright olive-green crown and back, a vivid yellow face, and a solid black throat and bib.
species.female: Similar but with a mottled, incomplete black throat and duller yellow face.
species.habitat: Coniferous and mixed forest, especially hemlock. Forages high in the canopy.

species.name: Willow Flycatcher
species.region: Reported on 19.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: American Tree Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 18.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Black-and-white Warbler
species.region: Reported on 17.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Forages like a nuthatch, creeping up and down and around tree trunks — the only warbler that does. Its song is a thin, squeaky 'weesa-weesa-weesa' like a rusty wheel turning.
species.male: Boldly striped black and white from head to tail, with a black throat and cheek.
species.female: Same zebra striping but with a white throat and a grey cheek — cleaner, less contrasty.
species.habitat: Deciduous and mixed forest. Works along trunks and large limbs rather than in the foliage.

species.name: Blackburnian Warbler
species.region: Reported on 17.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Purple Martin
species.region: Reported on 17.6% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: East of the Rockies, Purple Martins nest almost exclusively in human-supplied housing — a relationship that goes back to Indigenous peoples hanging hollow gourds. The eastern population is now entirely dependent on us.
species.male: Glossy deep blue-purple all over — the only North American swallow with a dark belly.
species.female: Grey-brown below with a pale collar and a duller, sooty-blue back.
species.habitat: Open areas near water, especially around martin houses and gourd racks in towns and farms.

species.name: Northern Rough-winged Swallow
species.region: Reported on 17.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Belted Kingfisher
species.region: Reported on 16.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Black-throated Blue Warbler
species.region: Reported on 16.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Blackpoll Warbler
species.region: Reported on 16.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Cape May Warbler
species.region: Reported on 14.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Scarlet Tanager
species.region: Reported on 15.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A shocking splash of red high in the summer canopy — and yet easy to miss, because it stays up top and sings a burry, hoarse song often described as a robin with a sore throat.
species.male: Blazing scarlet body with jet-black wings and tail. Nothing else looks remotely like it.
species.female: Olive-yellow with darker olive wings — completely different from the male, and easy to overlook.
species.habitat: Mature deciduous forest, especially oaks. Forages high in the canopy.

species.name: Swamp Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 14.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Wetland specialist with unusually long legs for a sparrow, letting it wade in shallow water and pick invertebrates from the surface — a niche no other sparrow occupies.
species.male: Rich rufous cap, a plain grey face, a white throat, and rusty wings. Overall the most colorful of the streaky sparrows.
species.female: Very similar, often with a more streaked crown and slightly duller tones.
species.habitat: Cattail marshes, sedge meadows, and wet shrubby areas. Rarely far from water.

species.name: Acadian Flycatcher
species.region: Reported on 14.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Brown Thrasher
species.region: Reported on 13.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Holds the largest song repertoire of any North American bird — over 1,100 song types. Unlike the mockingbird, which repeats each phrase three or more times, the thrasher usually sings each one twice.
species.male: Rich rufous above with a heavily black-streaked white breast, a long curved bill, and staring yellow eyes.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both thrash through leaf litter with the bill, hence the name.
species.habitat: Dense thickets, hedgerows, and shrubby field edges. Sings from an exposed high perch.

species.name: Prothonotary Warbler
species.region: Reported on 14.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: White-crowned Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 13.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Young males learn their song dialect from neighbors, producing local accents that differ from valley to valley. Migrating birds can stay awake and flying for two weeks straight.
species.male: Bold black-and-white striped crown, a clean grey face and breast, and a bright pinkish-orange bill.
species.female: Identical to the male. First-year birds have rusty-brown and tan crown stripes instead of black and white.
species.habitat: Brushy fields, hedgerows, chaparral, and gardens. Abundant in the West in winter.

species.name: Wilson's Warbler
species.region: Reported on 12.7% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Canada Warbler
species.region: Reported on 12.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Least Flycatcher
species.region: Reported on 11.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Ovenbird
species.region: Reported on 11.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Named for its domed, oven-shaped nest built right on the forest floor. The ringing 'TEACHER-TEACHER-TEACHER' crescendo is one of the loudest sounds in the eastern woods for a bird that looks like a small thrush.
species.male: Olive-brown above, boldly spotted below, with a bold white eye-ring and an orange crown stripe bordered in black.
species.female: Identical to the male. Walks — rather than hops — along the forest floor.
species.habitat: Interior deciduous forest with an open leaf-littered floor. Needs large unfragmented woods.

species.name: Yellow-throated Warbler
species.region: Reported on 11.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Creeps along branches like a nuthatch, probing bark and pine needle clusters. One of the earliest warblers to return north each spring, sometimes in February on the Gulf Coast.
species.male: Grey above, with a brilliant yellow throat, a bold black-and-white face pattern, and black side streaks.
species.female: Identical to the male, perhaps marginally duller.
species.habitat: Pine woods, cypress swamps, and sycamore-lined rivers in the Southeast.

species.name: Veery
species.region: Reported on 10.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Blue-headed Vireo
species.region: Reported on 9.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Brown Creeper
species.region: Reported on 8.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Spirals up a trunk from the base, then drops to the bottom of the next tree and starts again. So perfectly bark-camouflaged that it often goes unnoticed until it moves. Roosts tucked behind a slab of loose bark.
species.male: Streaky brown above and silvery-white below, with a thin decurved bill and a stiff tail used as a prop.
species.female: Identical to the male. Its high, thin song is at the very top of human hearing.
species.habitat: Mature forest with big rough-barked trees. Needs loose bark for its hammock-shaped nest.

species.name: Eastern Meadowlark
species.region: Reported on 9.5% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: The state bird of six states. Its flute-like, gurgling song is the defining sound of the western prairie — and Audubon named it 'neglecta' because everyone before him had overlooked it as just an Eastern Meadowlark.
species.male: Brilliant yellow underparts crossed by a bold black V on the chest; cryptic brown streaked back.
species.female: Identical to the male but slightly duller and smaller.
species.habitat: Grassland, prairie, pasture, and hayfields. Sings from fenceposts and wires.

species.name: Hermit Thrush
species.region: Reported on 9.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Sings what many consider the most beautiful song of any North American bird — a clear flute-like phrase, repeated at a different pitch each time. It can sing two notes at once using both halves of its syrinx.
species.male: Warm brown above with a rusty tail it flicks upward, a spotted breast, and a bold white eye-ring.
species.female: Identical to the male. The rusty tail contrasting with the browner back is the key mark.
species.habitat: Coniferous and mixed forest floor in summer; woodland understory and thickets in winter.

species.name: Orchard Oriole
species.region: Reported on 9.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Lincoln's Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 8.3% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Northern Mockingbird
species.region: Reported on 7.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Can learn 200 songs in a lifetime, imitating other birds, frogs, car alarms, and squeaky gates. Unmated males sing through the night, often from a streetlight, to the despair of anyone trying to sleep.
species.male: Grey above, pale below, with two white wingbars and huge white wing patches that flash in flight.
species.female: Identical to the male. Both sexes aggressively defend territory, dive-bombing cats and people alike.
species.habitat: Suburbs, parks, hedgerows, and open country with scattered shrubs. Thrives around people.

species.name: Northern Waterthrush
species.region: Reported on 8.2% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Yellow-throated Vireo
species.region: Reported on 8.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Blue-winged Warbler
species.region: Reported on 6.9% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Fox Sparrow
species.region: Reported on 7.1% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Red-breasted Nuthatch
species.region: Reported on 7.0% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Smears sticky conifer resin around its nest hole — possibly to deter predators and insects — and then must dive through the gap without touching the sides. In poor cone years it 'irrupts' far south of its normal range.
species.male: Blue-grey above, rusty-orange below, with a black cap and a bold white eyebrow over a black eye-line.
species.female: Same pattern but with a grey cap and paler, buffier underparts.
species.habitat: Coniferous and mixed forest. Wanders widely into deciduous woods and feeders during irruption years.

species.name: Rock Pigeon
species.region: Reported on 7.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: Domesticated for thousands of years as food, messenger, and racer, then gone feral worldwide. Navigates using the sun, the Earth's magnetic field, and possibly infrasound — a genuinely remarkable animal we mostly ignore.
species.male: Endlessly variable, but classically blue-grey with a white rump, two black wingbars, and an iridescent green-purple neck.
species.female: Nearly identical, with slightly less neck iridescence. Both sexes feed young on 'crop milk'.
species.habitat: Cities, bridges, farmyards, and grain elevators — skyscrapers substitute for ancestral cliffs.

species.name: White-eyed Vireo
species.region: Reported on 7.4% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).
species.about: A skulker of dense thickets with a loud, explosive, oddly mechanical song that often starts and ends with a sharp 'chick!'. Frequently mimics snatches of other species' calls.
species.male: Olive above with yellow 'spectacles', two white wingbars, yellow flanks, and a startling white iris.
species.female: Identical to the male, white iris and all. Juveniles have dark eyes that pale over the first year.
species.habitat: Dense shrubby thickets, briar tangles, and overgrown fields. Heard far more often than seen.

species.name: Yellow-billed Cuckoo
species.region: Reported on 6.8% of Ohio checklists at peak season (eBird).

species.name: Hooded Warbler

species.about: Nearly extirpated by 1900, then restored by one of the great wildlife comebacks — trap-and-transfer programs put them back across the continent. Can run 25 mph and fly, briefly, at 55.
species.male: Iridescent bronze body, bare red-and-blue head with wattles and a fleshy snood, and a bristly 'beard' on the chest. Fans the tail in display.
species.female: Smaller, duller, and browner, with a bluish bare head and usually no beard. Far more cryptic than the male.
species.habitat: Mature forest next to clearings and fields. Forages on the ground; roosts in big trees at night.

species.about: An irruptive nomad — in years when the northern conifer cone crop fails, siskins pour south by the millions, then may vanish entirely the following winter. Can pack enough seed in its crop to survive a night at −70°F.
species.male: Heavily streaked brown and buff with subtle yellow flashes in the wings and tail, and a sharply pointed bill.
species.female: Identical to the male, typically with less yellow in the wing.
species.habitat: Conifer forest; in irruption years, feeders and weedy fields far to the south.

species.about: Roger Tory Peterson memorably described the male as looking 'like a sparrow dipped in raspberry juice'. Steadily losing ground to the introduced House Finch wherever the two overlap.
species.male: Raspberry-red wash over the whole head, breast and back — richer and more extensive than a House Finch.
species.female: Boldly streaked brown and white with a strong white eyebrow and dark cheek patch — a much crisper face than a female House Finch.
species.habitat: Coniferous and mixed forest; feeders and woodland edge in winter.

species.female: Mottled buff-brown throughout with a shorter pointed tail — beautifully camouflaged against stubble and grass.
species.habitat: Farmland with weedy ditches, hedgerows, and grassy cover. Depends on agricultural landscapes.
